text: Sodium-ion batteries (SIBs) are considered to be one of the potential electrochemical energy storage systems, but the high energy density is still hard to achieve due to the high structural strain caused by the (de)sodiation process. In this study, we designed a novel P2-layered cathode material Na0.7Li0.03[Mg0.15Li0.07Mn0.75]O2 (NMLMO), in which dual-site lithium substitution is realized, and Li ions are uniformly distributed in both transition-metal (TM) sites and alkali-metal (AM) sites. Based on the integrated theoretical simulations and experimental results, it is confirmed that TM-site lithium realizes the Na-O-Li synergistic state in the TMO2 layer, which effectively triggers the robust oxygen anionic redox, thus realizes high capacity. Meanwhile, AM-site lithium acts as the structural “pillar” within the LiO6 prisms, preventing phase transformation and maintaining the stability of the cathode structure. As a result, a specific discharge capacity as high as 266 mAh g−1 can be realized while maintaining the cathode material with near zero-strain characteristic within the wide operating voltage window of 1.5–4.6 V. Therefore, the strategy of dual-site lithium substitution realizes the sacrifice of energy and power density to material stability, which provides a possible solution to balance the performance of next-generation SIBs.

Based on the integrated theoretical simulations and experimental results, it is confirmed that TM-site lithium realizes the Na-O-Li synergistic state in the TMO2 layer, which effectively triggers the robust oxygen anionic redox, thus realizes high capacity. Meanwhile, AM-site lithium acts as the structural “pillar” within the LiO6 prisms, preventing phase transformation and maintaining the stability of the cathode structure. As a result, a specific discharge capacity as high as 266 mAh g−1 can be realized while maintaining the cathode material with near zero-strain characteristic within the wide operating voltage window of 1.5–4.6 V.
